agent1_snippets.title: Fahr Syndrome -- Evaluation
agent1_snippets.content: Laboratory Investigations : Routine lab results are within normal limits in a patient with Fahr disease. Any abnormality should prompt further investigations to rule out secondary causes of brain calcifications. Routine hemogram and complete metabolic panel Blood and urine heavy metal levels Serum calcium, phosphorus, magnesium, alkaline phosphatase (ALP), calcitonin, vitamin D, parathyroid hormone (PTH) Cerebrospinal fluid analysis for bacteria, virus, and parasites Ellsworth Howard test (following 200 U PTH injection, 10 to 20 fold increase in urinary cAMP levels) Imaging studies : Bilateral basal ganglia calcification is visualized in both Fahr disease and secondary causes.

agent1_snippets.content: The patient is given oral doses of vitamin B12, and their 24-hour urine level of cobalamin is studied. [4] [5] [6]

agent1_snippets.title: Hypomagnesemia -- Evaluation
agent1_snippets.content: There is currently no clinically available "ionized" magnesium test. When in doubt, distinguishing between gastrointestinal and renal losses can be achieved by measuring the 24-hour urinary magnesium excretion. In addition, one can calculate the fractional excretion of magnesium (on a random urine specimen) with the following formula, where U and P refer to the urine and plasma concentrations of magnesium (Mg) and creatinine (Cr). FEMg = [(UMg x PCr) / (PMg x UCr x 0.7)] x 100 After confirming hypomagnesemia, typically obtain the etiology from the patient's history, which may include the mentioned causes. During the examination, look for vertical nystagmus and tetany. Check for the following signs: Chvostek sign: Tapping on facial nerve leads to twitching of facial muscles Trousseau sign: Carpopedal spasm induced by inflated blood pressure cuff

agent1_snippets.title: Ataxia -- Evaluation
agent1_snippets.content: The necessary tests are guided by clinical presentation and clinical suspicion. Blood tests for specific deficiencies, drugs, and toxins may be in order. Urinalysis can look for mercury level measurement.

agent1_snippets.title: Hand Nerve Compression Syndromes(Archived) -- Evaluation
agent1_snippets.content: Serologic studies : There are no blood tests used to support the diagnosis of nerve compression specifically, but the use of these tests may be necessary for medical conditions that can either promote nerve compression or mimic its symptoms. Some of the most frequently encountered conditions include diabetes and hypothyroidism. The assessment of a patient’s fasting blood glucose, hemoglobin A1c, or thyroid function tests may be helpful in the general management of the patient. Other conditions that could mimic nerve compression include deficiency of vitamin B12 or folate, vasculitides, and fibromyalgia. [1] [7] [14] [12]

agent1_snippets.title: Hypocalcemia -- Pearls and Other Issues
agent1_snippets.content: Always repeat calcium levels and check albumin levels to confirm hypocalcemia by correcting calcium levels for low albumin. Magnesium and vitamin D levels should be checked with hypocalcemia since these are essential and correctable causes.

agent1_snippets.title: Pseudologia Fantastica -- Evaluation
agent1_snippets.content: Psychological testing, including personality assessments and cognitive evaluations, helps to uncover underlying psychopathology and assess mental functioning. Comprehensive blood work is essential to rule out medical conditions and substance use, including liver and kidney function tests, electrolytes, complete blood count, vitamin B12 levels, folic acid and vitamin D levels, urinalysis, and urine toxicology. Neurological assessments and imaging studies can investigate potential brain dysfunction or neurological conditions contributing to the individual's lying behavior. Collateral information from family members, friends, or healthcare providers is crucial for verifying the individual's history and gaining additional insights into their behavior.

agent1_snippets.title: Lumbar Spinal Stenosis -- Differential Diagnosis
agent1_snippets.content: The differential diagnosis of LSS includes the following: Vascular claudication: presents with bilateral symptoms, pain while standing, and positive shopping cart sign; vascular imaging and ankle-brachial index are critical in the diagnosis Peripheral neuropathy: characteristically has stocking-and-glove pain distribution present even at rest and can also disturb sleep Lumbar spondylosis: positive straight leg or Lasègue test (L4 to S1) and reverse straight leg or Ely test (L2 to L4) are diagnostic for this condition Lumbar plexopathies: may present with sensorimotor deficits without pain, depending on the etiology Hip or knee osteoarthritis: usually present with pain and tenderness in the hip or knee area; neurologic signs are often absent Metabolic neuropathies: examples are alcohol abuse disorder and vitamin deficiencies; present with sensorimotor deficits and systemic signs, depending on the etiology A thorough evaluation can distinguish these conditions from LSS.

agent1_snippets.title: Polyarticular Arthritis -- Evaluation -- Laboratory Studies
agent1_snippets.content: In general, a complete blood count (CBC) and comprehensive metabolic panel (CMP) will help identify the presence of any systemic disorders contributing to the presentation. The clinical assessment should guide the selection of other tests and may include a urinalysis to screen for occult renal disease, thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) levels, serum 25-OH vitamin D levels, and viral serologies, eg, hepatitis B, hepatitis C, HIV, and parvovirus. A serum uric acid level may help support a diagnosis of gout, but it can be falsely low during a flare.

agent1_snippets.title: Thallium Toxicity -- Prognosis
agent1_snippets.content: Early diagnosis and treatment portend a better prognosis. [15] Neurologic damage may persist if gastrointestinal decontamination therapy is delayed. Patients can have signs and symptoms of peripheral neuropathy for many years after poisoning. Reports of persistent findings most commonly involve painful neuropathy of the lower extremities.

agent1_snippets.content: Knowledge of biohazard levels must not be limited to laboratory professionals. For instance, signs and symbols should always be posted on medical floors when dealing with a high-level biohazard.

agent1_snippets.title: Schilling Test (Archived) -- Potential Diagnosis
agent1_snippets.content: Patients with an abnormally low level of cobalamin in the urine in stage 1 usually undergo stage 2. If stage 2 shows a normal level of excreted vitamin B12, it means that the patient has low intrinsic factor levels, and one possible cause is pernicious anemia. If the test still indicates a low level of vitamin B12 in the urine, it means that the patient has a poor intestinal absorption of cobalamin. Further tests should be done to assess the cause of malabsorption, which can include Crohn disease, small bowel bacterial overgrowth syndrome, and infection by Diphyllobothrium latum.

agent1_snippets.title: Dimercaprol -- Indications -- Mercury
agent1_snippets.content: Acute or chronic elemental mercury exposure with a whole blood mercury concentration or 24-hour urine concentration of ≥ 100 mcg/L Acute inorganic mercury poisoning when whole blood mercury concentration or 24-hour urine concentration is greater than or equal to 100 mcg/L

agent1_snippets.title: Urolithiasis -- Treatment / Management
agent1_snippets.content: Outpatient management can be assisted by testing to determine the etiology of urolithiasis which includes testing focused on abnormalities in the serum (serum calcium, phosphorus, oxalate, sulfate, magnesium, citrate, cysteine, ammonium, vitamin D levels, lactate dehydrogenase, and parathyroid hormone) and the urine (urine electrolytes, pH, uric acid, creatinine, and calcium). These tests allow for further stone analysis to improve further management. [21]

agent1_snippets.title: Acrodynia -- Evaluation
agent1_snippets.content: Before commencing the workout, a thorough clinical history and an evaluation of the possible exposures in the domestic surroundings and the workplace are crucial. The laboratory evaluation should include the demonstration of mercury levels in the blood, urine, and body tissues. The initial exercise should consist of radiological evaluation, toxicological screening of blood and urine, basic metabolic panel, liver function tests, and complete blood profile. Spot urine collections can be used for creatinine estimations, but a 24-hour urine sample should estimate the urine mercury concentrations. [23]

agent2_snippets.content: Vitamin D and analogues is a pharmacological class that encompasses both natural vitamin D2 (ergocalciferol) and synthetic compounds such as dihydrotachysterol; dihydrotachysterol is identified as a member of the Vitamin D and analogues group, and vitamin D2 is likewise included within this broader category. agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1458
agent2_snippets.content: اضطراب السبيل البولي (urinary tract disorder) is recognized as having a broader relationship with قصور الكلى (renal failure), indicating that disturbances of the urinary system are often linked to or may progress to impaired kidney function. In this clinical context, magnesium gluconate is contraindicated in patients with قصور الكلى, meaning that the use of this magnesium supplement should be avoided in individuals who have renal failure to prevent potential adverse effects. Understanding the connection between urinary tract disorders and renal failure, as well as the medication contraindication, is essential for safe patient management and appropriate therapeutic decision‑making.

agent2_snippets.content: Acute and unspecified renal failure, which is classified as فشل كلوي حاد, is clinically associated with الحماض (acidosis) and فرطُ بوتاسيوم الدَّم (hyperkalemia). agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1592
agent2_snippets.content: Parathormon is classified within the functional group “hormones by function” and its disposition is that of a hormone. As a hormone, parathormon produces a specific physiologic effect: it leads to decreased renal Ca++ excretion. This reduction in renal calcium loss is a key component of parathormon’s role in maintaining calcium homeostasis, and recognizing that parathormon belongs to the hormone category and exerts this effect is important for the clinical management of calcium‑related disorders.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1926
agent2_snippets.content: Acidosis (الحماض) and hyperkalemia (فرطُ بوتاسيوم الدَّم) are medical conditions that frequently co‑occur. In clinical practice, the presence of an acid‑base disturbance such as metabolic or respiratory acidosis is often associated with elevated serum potassium levels, and conversely, high potassium concentrations can exacerbate or signal underlying acidotic states. Recognizing this interrelationship is important for accurate diagnosis, monitoring, and therapeutic management of patients who present with either condition.

agent2_snippets.content: Vitamin K 2 and vitamin K 3 are both classified as vitamins.
